name: Mega Stress Test
description: Maximum complexity scenario: 8 modules, 8 agents, 20 tasks, diamond+parallel deps, every feature exercised — lessons, search, file actions, config, hooks, graph analytics, escalations, reservation conflicts, export/import. 15 cycles.

plans.name: platform-v2-plan
plans.summary: Platform V2 — full rewrite with 20 tasks across 8 modules, 8 agents

cycles.title: Project kickoff — config, hooks, architecture
cycles.scene: Grace leads architecture while Frank bootstraps infrastructure. Config and hooks are verified first.

actions.content:
Platform V2 Architecture
========================
Services: auth-core, user-mgmt, api-layer, event-bus, data-store, search-idx, frontend-app
Communication: REST (sync) + Kafka (async)
Auth: OAuth2 + SAML federation, JWT with RS256
Data: PostgreSQL (primary) + Redis (cache) + Elasticsearch (search)
Infra: Kubernetes, Terraform, GitHub Actions

Dependency graph (diamond pattern):
  architecture-design
    -> setup-database + setup-event-bus + setup-auth (parallel fan-out)
      -> build-user-mgmt + build-api-events (converge on setup)
        -> build-api-endpoints (converge on user-mgmt + auth)
          -> build-frontend + security-audit (parallel)
            -> integration-tests (diamond converge)
              -> chaos-tests + load-tests
                -> canary-deploy -> full-deploy -> post-launch

Kubernetes Namespace Config
===========================
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: platform-v2
  labels:
    team: platform
    env: staging
---
Resource quotas:
  CPU: 16 cores
  Memory: 32Gi
  Pods: 50

Network policies:
  - Allow ingress from api-gateway only
  - Allow egress to data-store, event-bus
  - Deny all other inter-namespace traffic

actions.body: Architecture finalized. Auth-core: OAuth2 + SAML with RS256 JWTs. See docs/architecture/system-design.txt. You're on setup-auth once this cycle closes.
Data layer: PostgreSQL primary, Redis cache, Elasticsearch for search. Connection pooling via PgBouncer. You'll own setup-database and build-search-index.

cycles.title: Architecture complete, triple fan-out begins
cycles.scene: Architecture closes. Three foundation tasks unblock simultaneously — database, event bus, and auth.

actions.content:
PostgreSQL Schema
=================
users (id UUID PK, email UNIQUE, name, created_at, updated_at)
roles (id UUID PK, name UNIQUE, description)
user_roles (user_id FK, role_id FK, granted_at)
permissions (id UUID PK, resource, action, role_id FK)
sessions (id UUID PK, user_id FK, token_hash, expires_at, created_at)
oauth_tokens (id UUID PK, user_id FK, provider, access_token_enc, refresh_token_enc, expires_at)

Indexes: users(email), sessions(token_hash), sessions(expires_at), oauth_tokens(user_id, provider)
Connection pool: PgBouncer, max 100 connections, transaction mode

Kafka Topics
============
platform.users.created (key: user_id, value: UserCreatedEvent)
platform.users.updated (key: user_id, value: UserUpdatedEvent)
platform.auth.login (key: user_id, value: LoginEvent)
platform.auth.logout (key: user_id, value: LogoutEvent)
platform.api.request (key: request_id, value: ApiRequestEvent)
platform.search.reindex (key: entity_type, value: ReindexCommand)
platform.dlq (dead-letter queue for failed processing)

Partitions: 12 per topic
Replication: 3
Retention: 7 days (30 days for DLQ)
Schema: Avro with backward compatibility

OAuth2 Authorization Code Flow
===============================
1. Client redirects to /auth/authorize?provider=google&redirect_uri=...
2. Server generates state + PKCE challenge, stores in session
3. Redirect to Google with client_id, redirect_uri, state, code_challenge
4. Google redirects back with authorization code
5. Server exchanges code for tokens (POST to token endpoint)
6. Validate id_token, extract claims
7. Create/update local user, generate JWT (RS256, 1h expiry)
8. Set httpOnly secure cookie with JWT
9. Emit platform.auth.login event

SAML Federation (enterprise):
  SP-initiated flow via /auth/saml/login
  IdP metadata cached with 24h TTL
  Assertion validation: signature + conditions + audience

cycles.title: Database bug — connection pool exhaustion
cycles.scene: Dave hits a connection pool bug during stress testing the schema setup.

actions.body: CRITICAL: PgBouncer connection pool exhausted under concurrent migration. 100 connections maxed out — migrations deadlocked. Need to increase pool or serialize migrations.

actions.reason: Connection pool exhaustion during concurrent migrations

actions.content:
Debug: Connection Pool Exhaustion
==================================
Symptom: Migrations deadlock when run concurrently from CI
Root cause: Each migration opens a transaction, PgBouncer in
  transaction mode holds connection for duration. 5 parallel
  CI jobs x 20 connections each = 100 (pool max).

Fix:
  1. Run migrations serially (not in parallel CI jobs)
  2. Use advisory locks: SELECT pg_advisory_lock(12345)
  3. Increase pool to 200 for migration phase, drop back to 100

Chosen: Option 1 + 2 (advisory locks + serial execution)
Lesson: Never run DDL migrations concurrently against pooled connections

actions.body: Good analysis. Use advisory locks + serial migration. Capture this as a lesson.

actions.command: lesson add "PgBouncer pool exhaustion during migrations" --problem "Concurrent CI jobs running DDL migrations exhausted PgBouncer connection pool (100 max). Migrations deadlocked." --solution "Use pg_advisory_lock to serialize migrations. Never run DDL concurrently against connection-pooled databases." --learned "Connection poolers in transaction mode hold connections for entire transaction duration. DDL migrations must be serialized." --pattern "database-pooling"

cycles.title: Event bus schema conflict — reservation clash
cycles.scene: Alice needs to update event schemas for auth events, but Bob has the events directory reserved.

actions.body: BLOCKED: Need to add auth event schemas to src/events/ but Bob has exclusive reservation. Auth login/logout events need Avro schemas before I can finish setup-auth.
Alice needs src/events/ for auth schemas. Can you release and coordinate?

actions.command: lesson add "Coordinate shared directories via granular reservations" --problem "Exclusive reservation on entire src/events/ blocked another agent who needed a subdirectory" --solution "Use granular glob patterns (src/events/auth-*) instead of broad directory locks. Coordinate via messages before reserving shared paths." --learned "Broad exclusive reservations create unnecessary contention. Prefer narrow globs that match only the files you actually need." --pattern "reservation-strategy"

cycles.title: Foundation complete, second wave begins
cycles.scene: Database, event bus, and auth all close. Multiple tasks unblock — the complexity ramps up.

actions.body: User-mgmt will emit UserCreated and UserUpdated events. Make sure the event bus consumer handles both. Schema is in src/events/.

cycles.title: Parallel implementation sprint
cycles.scene: Four agents work simultaneously. Files are written, lessons referenced, messages fly.

actions.content:
RBAC Implementation
====================
Roles: admin, editor, viewer, billing
Permissions: resource:action pairs
  users:read, users:write, users:delete
  projects:read, projects:write
  billing:read, billing:manage

Enforcement:
  1. JWT contains role claims
  2. Middleware extracts roles from JWT
  3. Permission check: does any assigned role grant the required resource:action?
  4. Cache permission matrix in Redis (5min TTL)

Hierarchy: admin > editor > viewer (implicit inheritance)

API Event Producers
====================
Every mutation endpoint emits an event:
  POST /users -> UserCreatedEvent
  PUT /users/:id -> UserUpdatedEvent
  DELETE /users/:id -> UserDeletedEvent
  POST /auth/login -> LoginEvent
  POST /auth/logout -> LogoutEvent

Producer config:
  acks: all (wait for all replicas)
  retries: 3 with exponential backoff
  idempotency: enabled (exactly-once semantics)
  DLQ routing: after 3 failed retries

Schema validation: Avro schema registry check before produce

Grafana Dashboards
==================
1. Platform Overview: request rate, error rate, p50/p95/p99 latency
2. Auth Service: login rate, MFA usage, token refresh rate, failed attempts
3. Event Bus: produce rate, consume lag, DLQ size, schema errors
4. Database: connection pool usage, query latency, replication lag
5. Search: index rate, query latency, index size, shard health
6. Infrastructure: CPU, memory, disk, network by pod

cycles.title: Search index + API endpoints converge
cycles.scene: Dave finishes search indexing. Alice builds API endpoints. The dependency graph converges.

actions.content:
Elasticsearch Index Mapping
============================
Index: platform-users-v1
Mapping:
  user_id: keyword
  email: keyword + text (for partial match)
  name: text (standard analyzer)
  roles: keyword (array)
  created_at: date
  updated_at: date

Index: platform-content-v1
  content_id: keyword
  title: text (english analyzer)
  body: text (english analyzer)
  author_id: keyword
  tags: keyword (array)
  published_at: date

Indexing pipeline: Kafka consumer on platform.search.reindex
Bulk index: batches of 500, flush every 5s
Refresh interval: 1s (near real-time search)

API Routes
==========
REST:
  POST   /auth/login          (public)
  POST   /auth/logout         (authenticated)
  POST   /auth/refresh        (authenticated)
  GET    /auth/saml/login     (public, enterprise)
  POST   /auth/saml/callback  (public, enterprise)
  GET    /users               (admin, editor)
  POST   /users               (admin)
  GET    /users/:id           (authenticated, own profile or admin)
  PUT    /users/:id           (authenticated, own profile or admin)
  DELETE /users/:id           (admin)
  GET    /search?q=           (authenticated)

GraphQL:
  query { users, user(id), search(query) }
  mutation { createUser, updateUser, deleteUser }

Rate limits: 100 req/min (authenticated), 20 req/min (public)
Versioning: Accept header (application/vnd.platform.v2+json)

cycles.title: Security audit with findings
cycles.scene: Eve runs the security audit and finds two issues. Meanwhile, Carol starts the frontend.

actions.content:
Frontend Architecture
=====================
Framework: React 18 + TypeScript strict
State: React Query (server state) + Zustand (client state)
Routing: React Router v6
Styling: Tailwind CSS + Radix UI primitives
Auth: httpOnly cookie (automatic), token refresh on 401
i18n: react-intl with lazy-loaded locale bundles
Accessibility: WCAG 2.1 AA target

Pages:
  /login — OAuth + SAML login
  /dashboard — overview metrics
  /users — user management (admin)
  /profile — own profile
  /search — full-text search
  /settings — preferences, MFA enrollment

actions.body: Security finding #1 (HIGH): JWT RS256 key stored in environment variable as plaintext. Should use key vault (AWS Secrets Manager or HashiCorp Vault). Rotate immediately.
Security finding #2 (MEDIUM): Event bus consumer deserializes without schema validation. Malformed event could cause consumer crash. Add schema registry validation on consume.
Security audit found 2 issues: (1) HIGH — JWT signing key in plaintext env var, (2) MEDIUM — event consumer missing schema validation. Both need fixes before deployment.
Acknowledged. Alice will move JWT key to Vault, Bob will add schema validation. Track as lessons.

actions.command: lesson add "JWT signing keys must use secret management" --problem "RS256 JWT signing key stored as plaintext environment variable — accessible to any process on the host" --solution "Store signing keys in HashiCorp Vault or AWS Secrets Manager. Rotate keys quarterly. Use key ID (kid) in JWT header for rotation support" --learned "Never store cryptographic keys in environment variables. Use dedicated secret management with audit logging and rotation policies" --pattern "secrets-management"

lesson add "Validate event schemas on consume, not just produce" --problem "Kafka consumer deserialized events without schema registry validation — malformed event crashed consumer" --solution "Add Avro schema validation on both produce AND consume paths. Configure consumer to reject unknown schemas. Route failures to DLQ" --learned "Schema validation is a two-sided contract. Producing valid data isn't enough — consumers must also validate to handle schema evolution and corrupt messages" --pattern "event-driven"

cycles.title: Frontend escalation — SSR complexity
cycles.scene: Carol hits a blocker with server-side rendering and auth cookies.

actions.body: BLOCKED: SSR breaks auth flow. Server-side fetch can't access httpOnly cookies from the browser. Options: (1) skip SSR for authenticated pages, (2) forward cookies via middleware, (3) use BFF pattern. Need architecture decision.
Use BFF (Backend-for-Frontend) pattern: SSR server forwards cookies to API. Keeps httpOnly security while enabling SSR. See auth-architecture doc for flow.

actions.content:
BFF Pattern for SSR + Auth
===========================
Problem: Server-side fetch can't access httpOnly browser cookies
Solution: BFF proxy forwards cookies from incoming request

Flow:
  1. Browser sends request with httpOnly JWT cookie
  2. Next.js middleware extracts cookie from request headers
  3. SSR fetch to API includes cookie in Authorization header
  4. API validates JWT as normal
  5. SSR renders with authenticated data
  6. Client hydration continues with same cookie

Security: BFF runs same-origin, cookie never exposed to client JS
Performance: adds ~5ms latency per SSR request (acceptable)

actions.command: lesson add "BFF pattern for SSR with httpOnly cookies" --problem "Server-side rendering cannot access httpOnly cookies set by the browser — auth fails during SSR fetch" --solution "Use Backend-for-Frontend pattern: SSR middleware extracts cookie from incoming request and forwards it to API calls. Cookie never exposed to client JS" --learned "httpOnly cookies and SSR are fundamentally at odds. BFF pattern resolves this cleanly while preserving cookie security" --pattern "frontend-auth"

cycles.title: Docs and knowledge search
cycles.scene: Grace writes documentation, searching existing lessons and artifacts for accuracy.

actions.content:
Incident Response Runbook
=========================
1. DETECT: Grafana alert fires -> PagerDuty notification
2. ACKNOWLEDGE: Ack within 5 min, join #incident channel
3. TRIAGE: Check dashboards, determine severity (S1/S2/S3)
4. MITIGATE:
   - Deploy issue: rollback via CI pipeline
   - DB issue: check connection pool (see lesson: PgBouncer pool exhaustion)
   - Auth issue: check JWT key rotation (see lesson: secrets management)
   - Event bus: check consumer lag and DLQ (see lesson: schema validation)
5. RESOLVE: Verify metrics, update status page
6. POST-MORTEM: Blameless RCA within 48h, capture lessons in fl

cycles.title: Integration tests + load tests in parallel
cycles.scene: Heidi runs integration tests while Frank runs load tests. Both report results.

actions.content:
Integration Test Results
========================
Auth flow (OAuth2): PASS (login, refresh, logout, MFA)
Auth flow (SAML): PASS (SP-initiated, IdP metadata cache)
User CRUD: PASS (create, read, update, delete, list)
RBAC enforcement: PASS (admin, editor, viewer boundaries)
Event production: PASS (all mutation events emitted)
Event consumption: PASS (schema validation, DLQ routing)
Search indexing: PASS (bulk index, near-real-time refresh)
Search queries: PASS (full-text, filters, pagination)
Frontend SSR: PASS (BFF cookie forwarding)
Error scenarios: PASS (401, 403, 404, 429, 500)

Total: 87/87 passing

Load Test Results
==================
Tool: k6
Duration: 30 min sustained

Results:
  Throughput: 3200 req/s (target: 2000) -- PASS
  P50 latency: 12ms -- PASS
  P95 latency: 45ms -- PASS
  P99 latency: 120ms -- PASS (target: <200ms)
  Error rate: 0.01% -- PASS (target: <0.1%)
  CPU usage: 65% peak -- PASS
  Memory: stable at 2.1GB -- PASS
  DB connections: 78/100 pool -- PASS

Bottleneck: Search queries P99 at 180ms under load
  Recommendation: Add search result caching (Redis, 30s TTL)

actions.body: Integration tests: 87/87 passing. Full coverage of auth, CRUD, events, search, SSR, error scenarios. Ready for chaos testing.
Load tests: 3200 req/s sustained (60% above target). P99 120ms. One recommendation: cache search results in Redis. Ready for production.

cycles.title: Chaos tests — simulating failures
cycles.scene: Heidi runs chaos engineering tests: pod kills, network partitions, disk pressure.

actions.content:
Chaos Test Results
===================
Test 1: Kill auth-core pod -> new pod in 8s, zero failed requests (connection draining) -> PASS
Test 2: Kill data-store pod -> circuit breaker activates in 2s, cached reads continue -> PASS
Test 3: Network partition (event-bus isolated) -> events buffered, replayed after partition heals -> PASS
Test 4: Disk pressure on search-idx -> index goes read-only, alert fires in 30s -> PASS
Test 5: Clock skew (5min) on auth-core -> JWT validation rejects future tokens -> PASS
Test 6: Cascading failure (kill 3 pods simultaneously) -> system degrades gracefully, recovers in 45s -> PASS

All 6 chaos scenarios PASSED. System is resilient.

actions.body: Chaos tests: 6/6 passed. System survives pod kills, network partitions, disk pressure, clock skew, and cascading failures. Recovery time < 45s in all cases.

cycles.title: Canary and full deployment
cycles.scene: Frank deploys to production with canary rollout, then full deployment.

actions.content:
Canary Deployment Log
=====================
10:00 - Canary deployed: 5% traffic routed to v2
10:15 - Metrics check: error rate 0.02% (baseline 0.03%) -- OK
10:30 - Metrics check: P99 latency 95ms (baseline 110ms) -- OK (improved!)
11:00 - 1h mark: no anomalies, all dashboards green
11:30 - Increased to 25% traffic
12:00 - 2h mark at 25%: stable, no regressions
12:30 - Approved for full rollout

Decision: PROCEED to full deployment

actions.body: Full deployment complete. 100% traffic on v2. All health checks passing. Monolith scaled down to standby (1 replica for rollback).

cycles.title: Post-launch review and lesson capture
cycles.scene: Grace leads the post-launch review. The team captures final lessons from the entire project.

actions.command: lesson add "Canary deployment catches latency improvements too" --problem "Team expected canary to only find regressions, almost missed that v2 was 15% faster — needed to update monitoring baselines" --solution "Canary dashboards should show improvement metrics alongside regression alerts. Update baselines after successful full deploy" --learned "Canary deployments are bidirectional — they catch both regressions AND improvements. Update monitoring baselines post-deploy to avoid false alerts" --pattern "deployment"

lesson add "Chaos engineering reveals recovery time, not just failure modes" --problem "Initial chaos test plan only checked pass/fail (did it survive?). Missed that recovery from cascading failure took 45s — longer than SLA" --solution "Add recovery time assertions to all chaos scenarios. Define SLA for recovery (e.g., <30s for single failure, <60s for cascading)" --learned "Surviving failure isn't enough — recovery speed matters. Chaos tests should assert on time-to-recovery, not just eventual recovery" --pattern "chaos-engineering"

cycles.title: Knowledge export and graph analysis
cycles.scene: Final cycle: export everything, run graph analytics, verify the complete knowledge graph.
